task: Scroll back through earlier output and select text with vim motions — no mouse. How do you enter copy mode?
reveal: Ctrl+s — then vim motions (h/j/k/l, w/b), `/` to search, `v` then `y` to select & yank, `q`/Esc to exit. WezTerm grabs Ctrl+s before the shell, so it never triggers flow-control freeze.

task: You want the focused pane to fill the whole window for a moment, then snap back to the layout. How?
reveal: Alt+z toggles pane zoom (TogglePaneZoomState). Press again to restore.

task: Reorder the panes in the current tab without closing any. How?
reveal: Alt+Shift+[ rotates counter-clockwise, Alt+Shift+] clockwise (RotatePanes). WezTerm has no directional swap, so cycling is how you reorder.

task: Grow or shrink the focused pane without the mouse. How?
reveal: Alt+Shift+H/J/K/L grows toward that side (3 cells per press; repeat to nudge).

task: Split the current pane so a new one opens to the right. How?
reveal: Alt+\ (SplitHorizontal) — `\` ≈ a vertical divider, so it splits into left|right panes.

task: Split the current pane so a new one stacks below. How?
reveal: Alt+- (SplitVertical) — `-` ≈ a horizontal divider, so the new pane sits below.

task: Move focus to the pane on the left/right/up/down — no mouse. How?
reveal: Alt+h/j/k/l (or Alt+arrows): ActivatePaneDirection. Same hjkl as vim and niri.

task: Close just the focused pane, leaving the rest of the tab. How?
reveal: Alt+x (CloseCurrentPane, no confirm).

task: Open a new tab, and close the current one. How?
reveal: Alt+t spawns a tab (current dir); Alt+w closes the current tab.

task: Jump straight to the Nth tab. How?
reveal: Alt+1 … Alt+9 (ActivateTab 0–8).

task: Step to the previous / next tab. How?
reveal: Alt+[ previous, Alt+] next. Watch out: Alt+Shift+[ / ] instead *rotate panes*.

task: Edit a long command line with vim motions (jump words, change-inside-quotes, etc.). How do you leave insert mode?
reveal: Esc enters vi command mode (`bindkey -v`). The prompt arrow turns bold-yellow ❮; press `i`/`a` to go back to insert.

task: The grey ghost-text suggestion is exactly what you want — accept it without reaching for →. How?
reveal: Ctrl+E accepts the whole suggestion (end-of-line); Ctrl+F accepts just the next word (forward-word).

task: Recall earlier commands that begin with what you've already typed. How?
reveal: Type a prefix, then ↑/↓ — bound to history-search-backward/forward, so they match on the prefix instead of walking the whole history.

task: Find and rerun an old command by fuzzy-typing a fragment of it. How?
reveal: Ctrl+R — fzf fuzzy history search.

task: Drop a file or directory path into the command you're typing by fuzzy-picking it. How?
reveal: Ctrl+T — fzf file/path picker, inserts at the cursor.

task: Jump into a subdirectory by fuzzy-picking it instead of typing the path. How?
reveal: Alt+C — fzf fuzzy-cd.

task: Change into a directory by typing only its path — no `cd` in front. How?
reveal: AUTO_CD is on: type the path (e.g. `~/dotfiles`) and Enter; zsh cd's into it.

task: Go up one or two directories without typing `cd ../..`. How?
reveal: `..` → cd .. and `...` → cd ../.. (aliases).

task: Run a command this once but keep it out of your shell history. How?
reveal: Prefix it with a single space (HIST_IGNORE_SPACE). The same leading-space trick is wired up in PowerShell here too.

task: You ran a command in another open terminal a moment ago — recall it here without switching windows. Can you?
reveal: Yes — SHARE_HISTORY writes and re-reads history live, so every open zsh shares one timeline; ↑ or Ctrl+R finds it right away.

task: Tab-completing and several matches exist — instead of cycling blindly, pick from a visible menu with the arrows. How?
reveal: Press Tab: `zstyle ':completion:*' menu select` turns the match list into a navigable menu — arrows/Tab move the highlight, Enter accepts.

task: Tab-complete a path without matching its case — type `down` and get `Downloads`. Does it work?
reveal: Yes — the completion matcher-list (m:{a-zA-Z}={A-Za-z}) makes completion case-insensitive, so lowercase input completes mixed-case names.

task: Jump to a directory you visit often by typing only part of its name. How?
reveal: z <partial> — jumps to the most "frecent" match (e.g. `z dot` → ~/dotfiles).

task: You're unsure which match `z` will land on — pick from the candidates interactively. How?
reveal: zi <partial> — lists the matches in fzf to choose from.

task: Glance at the prompt only: are you in insert mode or vi command mode right now?
reveal: The prompt character says it: green/red ❯ = insert mode; bold-yellow ❮ = command mode (after Esc). The color change, not just the flipped arrow, signals the switch.

task: See how long the last command actually took. Where does it show?
reveal: On the right edge of the prompt — the cmd_duration module, shown only when the command ran ≥500ms (min_time).

task: The last command failed — read its exact exit code straight off the prompt. Where?
reveal: A red `✘<code>` just before the ❯ (the status module, shown only on failure) — e.g. ✘130 = Ctrl-C, ✘127 = command not found.

task: Without running `git status`, read the current branch and whether the tree is dirty straight off the prompt. Where?
reveal: Left of the prompt: git_branch (a branch glyph + name, purple) then git_status (red symbols for staged/modified/untracked). No branch shown = not in a repo.

task: The directory in the prompt reads `…/src/app` instead of the full path. What's the rule?
reveal: directory keeps the last 3 components (truncation_length = 3), and inside a git repo truncates to the repo root; the leading `…/` (truncation_symbol) flags that it was shortened.

task: You backgrounded a job with `sleep 30 &`. How does the prompt tell you one is still running?
reveal: A blue jobs glyph appears on the left prompt (shown once ≥1 job; number_threshold = 1) and vanishes when none remain.

task: See what time it is — and roughly when the last command ran — without a separate clock. Where on the prompt?
reveal: Far-right edge: the time module (right_format) shows HH:MM in 24h, dimmed so it stays in the background.

task: Before pip-installing, confirm which Python virtualenv is active straight from the prompt. Where?
reveal: The python module shows a Python glyph plus the active venv name (read free from $VIRTUAL_ENV, no subprocess). Nothing after the glyph = no venv, so you'd install globally.

task: You want Esc on the home row and Ctrl without reaching into the corner. What does CapsLock do now?
reveal: Tap CapsLock = Esc; hold CapsLock = Ctrl (overload). The Esc key in turn becomes CapsLock.

task: This laptop's physical Super key is dead. How do you still press niri's Mod (Super)?
reveal: Left Ctrl is remapped to Super (leftmeta). When you genuinely need Ctrl, hold CapsLock.

task: You edited keyd/default.conf — how do you apply it without a reboot?
reveal: sudo install -Dm644 keyd/default.conf /etc/keyd/default.conf && sudo keyd reload.

task: Move focus between tiled windows and columns. How?
reveal: Mod+H/J/K/L (or Mod+arrows): column-left / window-down / window-up / column-right.

task: Jump straight to workspace N. How?
reveal: Mod+1 … Mod+9 (focus-workspace); Mod+U/I move down/up; add Ctrl to drag the focused column along.

task: Move the focused window/column itself, not just the focus. How?
reveal: Add Ctrl to the focus keys: Mod+Ctrl+H/J/K/L.

task: Get a zoomed-out view of every workspace at once. How?
reveal: Mod+O toggles the overview.

task: Cycle the focused column through your preset widths (50 / 66 / 80%). How?
reveal: Mod+R (switch-preset-column-width); Mod+Shift+R cycles the other way.

task: Make the focused column fill the screen, then go true fullscreen. How?
reveal: Mod+F maximizes the column; Mod+Shift+F is fullscreen; Mod+M fills edge-to-edge with no gaps.

task: Pop the focused window out of the tiling layout so it floats. How?
reveal: Mod+V toggles floating; Mod+Shift+V jumps focus between the floating and tiling layers.

task: Pull the neighbouring window into the current column, or push one back out. How?
reveal: Mod+[ consume-or-expel left, Mod+] right; Mod+, / Mod+. pull into / push out of the column.

task: Stack a column's windows as tabs instead of tiling them down the screen. How?
reveal: Mod+W toggles the column's tabbed display.

task: Open a terminal (and the app launcher) from the compositor. How?
reveal: Mod+T spawns WezTerm; Mod+D opens the cosmic-launcher.

task: You forgot a niri binding — show the built-in cheat-sheet overlay. How?
reveal: Mod+Shift+/ (show-hotkey-overlay).

task: Close just the focused window. How?
reveal: Mod+Q (close-window). Distinct from WezTerm's Alt+x / Alt+w, which close panes/tabs inside the terminal.

task: On a multi-monitor setup, move focus to the monitor to the left/right/up/down. How?
reveal: Mod+Shift+H/J/K/L (or Mod+Shift+arrows): focus-monitor that way. Add Ctrl (Mod+Shift+Ctrl+…) to send the column to that monitor instead.

task: Send the focused column straight to workspace N, not just jump there yourself. How?
reveal: Mod+Ctrl+1 … Mod+Ctrl+9 (move-column-to-workspace N). Plain Mod+1…9 only moves your focus there.

task: The Mod+R presets aren't quite right — nudge the focused column's width in small steps. How?
reveal: Mod+Minus shrinks, Mod+Equal grows it by 10% (set-column-width ±10%). Mod+R cycles the 50/66/80% presets; these fine-tune off-preset.

task: Jump focus to the very first or very last column in the workspace. How?
reveal: Mod+Home → focus-column-first, Mod+End → focus-column-last. Add Ctrl (Mod+Ctrl+Home/End) to move the focused column there instead.

task: Take a screenshot — whole screen interactively, or just the focused window. How?
reveal: Print → cosmic-screenshot (interactive); Ctrl+Print → non-interactive full capture; Alt+Print → screenshot-window (niri's per-window grab). Saved under ~/Pictures/Screenshots.

task: Two split windows are open in Neovim — move focus between them without the mouse or a <C-w> prefix. How?
reveal: Ctrl+h/j/k/l jump to the split on that side (mapped to <C-w> h/j/k/l). Same hjkl-with-a-modifier as WezTerm's Alt+ and niri's Mod+, so the muscle memory carries across.

task: Split the current Neovim window — one below, one to the right. With leader = Space, how?
reveal: <leader>- (Space then -) splits below, <leader>| (Space then |) splits right; splitbelow/splitright make each open where the symbol suggests.

task: Yank a line in Neovim and paste it into the browser (and vice-versa) — no register gymnastics. Does plain y/p do it?
reveal: Yes — clipboard = unnamedplus routes every yank/delete/put through the system clipboard, so y here and Ctrl+V elsewhere share one buffer.

task: Make /todo also match Todo and TODO, but /Todo match only the capitalized one. What setting gives that?
reveal: ignorecase + smartcase: searches stay case-insensitive until your pattern contains an uppercase letter, then they turn case-sensitive.

task: Preview exactly what a :substitute will change before you press Enter. How?
reveal: Just start typing `:%s/old/new/` — inccommand = 'split' live-highlights every match and shows the result in a preview split as you type.

task: After a search the matches stay highlighted and it's noisy — clear the highlight without turning search highlighting off for good. How?
reveal: Press <Esc> in normal mode: it's mapped to :nohlsearch, so the highlight clears until your next search.

task: You opened a :terminal inside Neovim and want normal-mode motions to scroll its output. How do you leave terminal-insert mode?
reveal: Double-tap Esc (<Esc><Esc>, mapped from the awkward <C-\><C-n>) to drop into normal mode.

task: You closed a file yesterday and reopened it today — can you still press u to undo edits from that earlier session?
reveal: Yes — undofile persists each file's undo history to disk, so the whole undo tree survives closing and reopening Neovim.

task: Bump the terminal font up a notch, then snap it back to the default — no menu. How?
reveal: Ctrl+= grows it, Ctrl+- shrinks it, Ctrl+0 resets to the configured 11pt (Increase/Decrease/ResetFontSize). The window won't resize to follow — adjust_window_size_when_changing_font_size is off.

task: You saved wezterm.lua but the change hasn't shown up — force a config reload now. How?
reveal: Ctrl+Shift+R (ReloadConfiguration). WezTerm auto-reloads on save anyway; this is the manual nudge for when it hasn't caught up.

task: Several panes are open and there's no per-pane title bar — which one has focus right now?
reveal: The bright one: inactive panes are dimmed (inactive_pane_hsb brightness 0.65) and the split lines are coloured blue (#7aa2f7) so boundaries read as borders. The undimmed pane is the focused one.

task: You're midway through a rebase or merge — does the prompt say so, without running git status?
reveal: Yes — the git_state module shows it in bold yellow (REBASING, MERGING, CHERRY-PICKING, …) just after the branch/status, so you know a multi-step git op is still open.

task: Each time you focus a different column it glides to the middle of the screen instead of staying put. Why?
reveal: layout { center-focused-column "always" } — niri always re-centers whatever column you focus, so the active window sits mid-screen rather than drifting to an edge.

task: Switch between workspaces with the mouse wheel instead of the keyboard. How?
reveal: Hold Mod and scroll (Mod+WheelScrollUp/Down, 150ms cooldown). Add Ctrl (Mod+Ctrl+wheel) to drag the focused column along to the next workspace as you scroll.

task: A column stacks two windows — resize their heights, not the column's width. How?
reveal: Mod+Shift+Minus / Mod+Shift+Equal shrink/grow the focused window's height by 10%; Mod+Ctrl+Shift+R cycles preset heights, Mod+Ctrl+R resets. (Plain Mod+Minus/Equal and Mod+R do column width.)

task: Let the focused column grow into whatever width the other columns aren't using — without hiding them. How?
reveal: Mod+Ctrl+F (expand-column-to-available-width). Unlike Mod+F (maximize) it only takes the leftover space, so neighbouring columns stay on screen.

task: A remote-desktop or VM window is swallowing your Mod shortcuts. How do you grab them back?
reveal: Mod+Escape toggles toggle-keyboard-shortcuts-inhibit — it's set allow-inhibiting=false so niri always hears that one chord, letting you flip the app's keyboard grab off (and on) again.

task: After you yank in Neovim, the copied text flashes for a moment. What does that, and why is it handy?
reveal: A TextYankPost autocmd calls vim.hl.on_yank() to briefly highlight the yanked region — instant confirmation of exactly what landed in the register, so you don't paste the wrong range.

task: You want `*` to match dotfiles too — e.g. `ls *` to include .gitignore — without typing the leading dot. Does it?
reveal: Yes — setopt GLOB_DOTS drops the "needs an explicit leading dot" rule, so `*` and tab-completion match hidden files like any other.

task: A command just failed for want of sudo, and you'd rather not retype it (or its long last argument). How?
reveal: History expansion: `!!` is the whole previous command — so `sudo !!`; `!$` is its last argument — `mkdir build && cd !$`; `!*` is all its args. They expand the moment you press Enter (BANG_HIST, on by default).

task: You cd'd deep into one tree, then into another — jump straight back to where you just were. How?
reveal: `cd -` toggles to the previous directory ($OLDPWD) and prints it. The `..`/`...` aliases go *up* the tree; `cd -` goes *back* to the last place you stood.

task: Half-way through a long line you spot a wrong word three back — fix it without holding Backspace. With vi mode on, how?
reveal: Press Esc for command mode, then edit with vim motions: `b`/`w`/`0`/`$` move, `cw` change a word, `dt/` delete up to the next `/`, `C` change to end of line; `i`/`a` drop back into typing. (zsh gives you the motions, not `ciw`-style text objects.)

task: Back up a file to name.bak, or create src/{api,db,web} at once — without typing the shared part twice. How?
reveal: Brace expansion: `cp config.toml{,.bak}` → two args sharing the stem; `mkdir -p src/{api,db,web}` makes all three; `{1..5}` → 1 2 3 4 5. It's expanded by the shell before the command runs.

task: You've typed a long line and want to scrap it — or just the last word — without mashing Backspace. How?
reveal: Ctrl+U kills the line (vi-kill-line), Ctrl+W deletes the word before the cursor, Ctrl+L clears the screen. All work straight from insert mode, no Esc needed.
